solution:
For no slipping, v0=ωR
Now, vA=vB=v02+(ωR)2
=2v0

⇒x=2

subject: Physics
q: Two masses $m$ and 2 m are connected by a light string going over a pulley (disc) of mass 30 m with radius $r=0.1 \mathrm{~m}$. The pulley is mounted in a vertical plane and it is free to rotate about its axis. The 2 m mass is released from rest and its speed when it has descended through a height of 3.6 m is $\_\_\_\_$ $\mathrm{m} / \mathrm{s}$. (Assume string does not slip and $\mathrm{g}=10 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}^{2}$)
question: Two masses $m$ and 2 m are connected by a light string going over a pulley (disc) of mass 30 m with radius $r=0.1 \mathrm{~m}$. The pulley is mounted in a vertical plane and it is free to rotate about its axis. The 2 m mass is released from rest and its speed when it has descended through a height of 3.6 m is $\_\_\_\_$ $\mathrm{m} / \mathrm{s}$. (Assume string does not slip and $\mathrm{g}=10 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}^{2}$)
solution:
Let the speed of the masses be $v$ and the angular velocity of the pulley be $\omega = v/r$ since the string does not slip.
The pulley is a disc of mass $M = 30m$ and radius $r$. Its moment of inertia is $I = \frac{1}{2}Mr^2 = \frac{1}{2}(30m)r^2 = 15mr^2$.
Using the principle of conservation of mechanical energy, the loss in potential energy of the system equals the gain in kinetic energy.
When the mass $2m$ descends by $h = 3.6$ m, the mass $m$ ascends by $h = 3.6$ m.
Loss in potential energy $\Delta U = (2m)gh - (m)gh = mgh$.
Gain in kinetic energy $\Delta K = \frac{1}{2}(2m)v^2 + \frac{1}{2}(m)v^2 + \frac{1}{2}I\omega^2$.
Substituting $I$ and $\omega$: $\Delta K = \frac{3}{2}mv^2 + \frac{1}{2}(15mr^2)(\frac{v}{r})^2 = \frac{3}{2}mv^2 + \frac{15}{2}mv^2 = 9mv^2$.
Equating $\Delta U = \Delta K$: $mgh = 9mv^2$.
$v^2 = \frac{gh}{9}$.
Given $g = 10$ m/s$^2$ and $h = 3.6$ m:
$v^2 = \frac{10 \times 3.6}{9} = \frac{36}{9} = 4$.
$v = \sqrt{4} = 2$ m/s.

subject: Physics
q: A solid sphere of mass ' $m$ ' and radius ' $r$ ' is allowed to roll without slipping from the highest point of an inclined plane of length ' $L$ ' and makes an angle $30^{\circ}$ with the horizontal. The speed of the particle at the bottom of the plane is $v_1$. If the angle of inclination is increased to $45^{\circ}$ while keeping $L$ constant. Then the new speed of the sphere at the bottom of the plane is $v_2$. The ratio $v_1^2: v_2^2$ is
question: A solid sphere of mass ' $m$ ' and radius ' $r$ ' is allowed to roll without slipping from the highest point of an inclined plane of length ' $L$ ' and makes an angle $30^{\circ}$ with the horizontal. The speed of the particle at the bottom of the plane is $v_1$. If the angle of inclination is increased to $45^{\circ}$ while keeping $L$ constant. Then the new speed of the sphere at the bottom of the plane is $v_2$. The ratio $v_1^2: v_2^2$ is
solution: using WET $\begin{aligned} & \mathrm{W}_{\mathrm{g}}=\mathrm{k}_{\mathrm{f}}-\mathrm{k}_{\mathrm{i}} \\ & \mathrm{Mg} \mathrm{~L} \sin \theta=\mathrm{k}_{\mathrm{f}}-\mathrm{k}_{\mathrm{i}} \end{aligned}$ K.E. in pure rolling $\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{mV}_{\mathrm{cm}}^2+\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{I}_{\mathrm{cm}} \omega^2$ $\begin{aligned} & =\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{mV}^2+\frac{1}{2} \times \frac{2}{5} \mathrm{mR}^2 \frac{\mathrm{~V}^2}{\mathrm{R}^2} \\ & \frac{7}{10} \mathrm{mV}^2 \end{aligned}$ $\mathrm{mgL} \sin \theta=\frac{7}{10} \mathrm{mV}_{\mathrm{f}}^2-0$ $\begin{aligned} & \mathrm{V}_{\mathrm{f}}^2 \propto \sin \theta \\ & \left(\frac{\mathrm{~V}_1}{\mathrm{~V}_2}\right)^2=\frac{\sin \theta_1}{\sin \theta_2}=\frac{\sin 30^{\circ}}{\sin 45^{\circ}}=\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \end{aligned}$

subject: Physics
q:
A huge circular arc of length 4.4 ly subtends an angle 4s at the centre of the circle. How long it would take for a body to complete 4 revolution if its speed is 8 AU per second?
Given : 1 ly=9.46×1015 m

1 AU=1.5×1011 m
question:
A huge circular arc of length 4.4 ly subtends an angle 4s at the centre of the circle. How long it would take for a body to complete 4 revolution if its speed is 8 AU per second?
Given : 1 ly=9.46×1015 m

1 AU=1.5×1011 m
solution:
ℓ=4.4ly=4.4×9.46×1015

length of Arc. =ℓ=Rθ

4.4×9.46×1015=Rθ

θ=4 S=4×4.843×10-6=1.94×10-5 rad

4.4×9.46×1015=R×1.94×10-5

R=2.1455×1021 meter

Speed =8Au=8×1.5×1011 ms-1=12×1011 ms-1

4 revolution means distance =4×2πR metre

time = distance  speed =4×2πR12×1011; time =8×3.14×2.1455×102112×1011⇒4.5×1010sec

q: The moment of inertia of a circular ring of mass $M$ and diameter r about a tangential axis lying in the plane of the ring is :
question: The moment of inertia of a circular ring of mass $M$ and diameter r about a tangential axis lying in the plane of the ring is :
solution:
Diameter is given as R.
$\begin{aligned}
& \therefore \text { Radius }=\mathrm{R} / 2 \\ & \mathrm{I}_{\text {tan gent }}=\frac{3}{2} \mathrm{~m}\left(\frac{\mathrm{R}}{2}\right)^2=\frac{3}{8} \mathrm{mR}^2
\end{aligned}$

question: A metre scale is balanced on a knife edge at its centre. When two coins, each of mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>10</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi></math> are put one on the top of the other at the <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>10</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>0</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math> mark the scale is found to be balanced at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>40</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>0</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math> mark. The mass of the metre scale is found to be <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><mi>kg</mi></math>. The value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math> is _____ .
solution:
Let the mass of the scale be m.

The centre of mass of the scale will be at 50 cm.

Applying torque balance, 

0=mg×50-40-2×10g×40-10⇒m=60 g=6×10-2 kg

subject: Physics
q: The coordinates of a particle with respect to origin in a given reference frame is \((1,1,1)\) meters. If a force of \(\overrightarrow{\mathrm{F}}=\hat{i}-\hat{j}+\hat{k}\) acts on the particle, then the magnitude of torque (with respect to origin) in z-direction is ______.
question: The coordinates of a particle with respect to origin in a given reference frame is \((1,1,1)\) meters. If a force of \(\overrightarrow{\mathrm{F}}=\hat{i}-\hat{j}+\hat{k}\) acts on the particle, then the magnitude of torque (with respect to origin) in z-direction is ______.
solution: $\begin{aligned} & \vec{\tau}=\overrightarrow{\mathrm{r}} \times \overrightarrow{\mathrm{F}}=\left|\begin{array}{ccc}\hat{\mathrm{i}} & \hat{\mathrm{j}} & \hat{\mathrm{k}} \\ 1 & 1 & 1 \\ 1 & -1 & 1\end{array}\right| \\ & \vec{\tau}=\hat{\mathrm{k}}(-1-1)=-2 \hat{\mathrm{k}} \\ & |\vec{\tau}|=2 \mathrm{Nm}\end{aligned}$

q: Angular momentum of a single particle moving with constant speed along circular path :
question: Angular momentum of a single particle moving with constant speed along circular path :
options: remains same in magnitude but changes in the direction
remains same in magnitude and direction
changes in magnitude but remains same in the direction
solution: The angular momentum is defined as the vector product of linear momentum and perpendicular distance, L→=r→×p→=r→×mv→, And direction will be upward & remain constant.

q: A uniform solid cylindrical roller of mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>m</mi></math> is being pulled on a horizontal surface with force <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>F</mi></math> parallel to the surface and applied at its centre. If the acceleration of the cylinder is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>a</mi></math> and it is rolling without slipping then the value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>F</mi></math> is:
question: A uniform solid cylindrical roller of mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>m</mi></math> is being pulled on a horizontal surface with force <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>F</mi></math> parallel to the surface and applied at its centre. If the acceleration of the cylinder is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>a</mi></math> and it is rolling without slipping then the value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>F</mi></math> is:
solution:
From free body diagram of cylinder

F-fs=ma .....(1)

∵   ∑ fext=macm

also ∑τext=Icmα

⟹fs R=Icmα

⟹fs R=12 mR2α ..... (2)

For rolling without slipping




a=Rα ...... (3)

⟹ α=aR

∴   fs R=12mR2aR

⟹ fs=12ma

Put in (1)

F-12ma=ma

⟹F=32ma

question: One solid sphere A and another hollow sphere B are of same mass and same outer radii. Their moment of inertia about their diameters are respectively $\mathrm{I}_{\mathrm{A}}$ and $\mathrm{I}_{\mathrm{B}}$ such that
options:
yes, $45^{\circ}$
$I_A / I_B=d_A / d_B$
Where $d_A$ and $d_B$ are their densities.
solution:
Moment of inertia of a uniform density solid sphere, $A=\frac{2}{5} M R^2$
Since $M$ and $R$ are same, $I_A < I_B$.

q: A spherical shell of 1 kg mass and radius R is rolling with angular speed ω on horizontal plane (as shown in figure). The magnitude of angular momentum of the shell about the origin O is a3R2ω. The value of a will be
question: A spherical shell of 1 kg mass and radius R is rolling with angular speed ω on horizontal plane (as shown in figure). The magnitude of angular momentum of the shell about the origin O is a3R2ω. The value of a will be
solution:
Angular momentum of a rotating and translating body is given by,

LO=mvR+Iω=mvR+23MR2ω

For rolling, v=ωR

  ⇒LO=mωR21+23=53mωR2

Therefore, the value of a=5.

subject: Physics
q:
A particle of mass m is moving in time t on a trajectory given by, 

r→=10αt2i^+5βt-5j^

where α and β are dimensional constants.

The angular momentum of the particle becomes the same as it was for t=0 at time t=_____ seconds.
question:
A particle of mass m is moving in time t on a trajectory given by, 

r→=10αt2i^+5βt-5j^

where α and β are dimensional constants.

The angular momentum of the particle becomes the same as it was for t=0 at time t=_____ seconds.
solution:
r→=10αt2i^+5βt-5j^

v→=20α+i^+5βj^

L→=mr→×v→

=m10αt2i^+5βt-5j^×20αi^+5βj^

L→=m50αβt2k^-100αβt2-5tk^

At t=0, L→=0→

50αβt2-100αβt2-5t=0

t-2t-5=0

t=10 sec

subject: Physics
q: A sphere of radius a and mass m rolls along a horizontal plane with constant speed v0. It encounters an inclined plane at angle θ and climbs upward. Assuming that it rolls without slipping, how far up the sphere will travel?
question: A sphere of radius a and mass m rolls along a horizontal plane with constant speed v0. It encounters an inclined plane at angle θ and climbs upward. Assuming that it rolls without slipping, how far up the sphere will travel?
solution:
From energy conservation
mgh=12mv02+12Iω2
⇒mgh=12mv02+12×25ma2×v02a2
⇒gh=12v02+15v02
⇒gh=710v02

From triangle, sin θ=hl
Then h=l sin θ
⇒l sin θ=710v02 g
Thus, the sphere will travel distance, l=710v02 g sin θ.

question: A solid sphere of mass <math><mi mathvariant="normal">M</mi></math> and radius <math><mi mathvariant="normal">R</mi></math> is divided into two unequal parts. The first part has a mass of <math><mfrac><mrow><mn>7</mn><mi mathvariant="normal">M</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>8</mn></mrow></mfrac></math> and is converted into uniform disc of radius <math><mi> </mi><mn>2</mn><mi mathvariant="normal">R</mi></math> . solution:
Moment of inertia of disc about the axis passing its centre and perpendicular to its surface, I1=7M82R22=7MR24.
Radius of small sphere r is related as
M43πR3=M843πr3
r=R2
Moment of inertia of sphere about its axis passing through its centre∴I2=25M8R22=MR280
I1I2=74×801=140

subject: Physics
q: Suppose there is a uniform circular disc of mass $M \mathrm{~kg}$ and radius $r \mathrm{~m}$ shown in figure. The shaded regions are cut out from the disc. The moment of inertia of the remainder about the axis $A$ of the disc is given by $\frac{x}{256} M r^{2}$. The value of $x$ is $\_\_\_\_$.
question: Suppose there is a uniform circular disc of mass $M \mathrm{~kg}$ and radius $r \mathrm{~m}$ shown in figure. The shaded regions are cut out from the disc. The moment of inertia of the remainder about the axis $A$ of the disc is given by $\frac{x}{256} M r^{2}$. The value of $x$ is $\_\_\_\_$.
solution:
The moment of inertia of the complete disc about axis A is
$I_{disc} = \frac{1}{2}Mr^2 = \frac{128Mr^2}{256}$.
Each removed circular region has radius $r/4$ and mass $m_i = M/(r^2/16r^2) = M/16$.
For each removed disc at distance $3r/4$ from axis A, using the parallel axis theorem:
$I_{removed} = \frac{1}{2}m_i(r/4)^2 + m_i(3r/4)^2 = \frac{Mr^2}{512} + \frac{9Mr^2}{256} = \frac{19Mr^2}{512}$.
For two removed discs:
$I_{2removed} = \frac{38Mr^2}{512} = \frac{19Mr^2}{256}$.
Therefore:
$I_{remainder} = \frac{128Mr^2}{256} - \frac{19Mr^2}{256} = \frac{109Mr^2}{256}$, so $x = 109$.

question: A string is wrapped around the rim of a wheel of moment of inertia $0.40 \mathrm{kgm}^2$ and radius $10 \mathrm{~cm}$. The wheel is free to rotate about its axis. Initially the wheel is at rest. The string is now pulled by a force of $40 \mathrm{~N}$. The angular velocity of the wheel after $10 \mathrm{~s}$ is $x \mathrm{rad} / \mathrm{s}$, where $x$ is _______
solution: $\begin{aligned} & \tau=\mathrm{FR}=\mathrm{I} \alpha \Rightarrow 40 \times 0.1=0.4 \alpha \\ & \alpha=10 \mathrm{rad} / \mathrm{s}^2 \\ & \mathrm{~W}_{\mathrm{f}}=10 \times 10=100 \mathrm{rad} / \mathrm{s}\end{aligned}$

$=-F(\hat{i}+\hat{j})$

q: A circular disc reaches from top to bottom of an inclined plane of length <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>L</mi><mo>.</mo></math> When it slips down the plane, it takes time  <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>t</mi><mn>1</mn></msub></math>. When it rolls down the plane, it takes time <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>t</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math>. The value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><msub><mi>t</mi><mn>2</mn></msub><msub><mi>t</mi><mn>1</mn></msub></mfrac></math> is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msqrt><mfrac><mn>3</mn><mi>x</mi></mfrac></msqrt></math>. The value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math> will be
question: A circular disc reaches from top to bottom of an inclined plane of length <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>L</mi><mo>.</mo></math> When it slips down the plane, it takes time  <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>t</mi><mn>1</mn></msub></math>. When it rolls down the plane, it takes time <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>t</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math>. The value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><msub><mi>t</mi><mn>2</mn></msub><msub><mi>t</mi><mn>1</mn></msub></mfrac></math> is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msqrt><mfrac><mn>3</mn><mi>x</mi></mfrac></msqrt></math>. The value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math> will be
solution:
If disk slips on inclined plane, then its acceleration, a1=gsinθ
L=12a1t12
⇒t1=2 La1 …i

If disk rolls on inclined plane, its acceleration,
a2=gsinθ1+ImR2
a2=gsinθ1+mR22 mR2
a2=23gsinθ
Now L=12a2·t22
⇒t2=2 La2 …ii

Now, t2t1=a1a2=32
⇒x=2

subject: Physics
q:
The position vectors of two 1 kg particles, (A) and (B), are given by
$\overrightarrow{\mathrm{r}}_{\mathrm{A}}=\left(\alpha_1 \mathrm{t}^2 \hat{i}+\alpha_2 \mathrm{t} \hat{j}+\alpha_3 \mathrm{t} \hat{k}\right) \mathrm{m}$ and $\overrightarrow{\mathrm{r}}_{\mathrm{B}}=\left(\beta_1 \mathrm{t} \hat{i}+\beta_2 \mathrm{t}^2 \hat{j}+\beta_3 \mathrm{t} \hat{k}\right) \mathrm{m}$, respectively; $\left(\alpha_1=1 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}^2, \alpha_2=3 \mathrm{n} \mathrm{m} / \mathrm{s}, \alpha_3=2 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}, \beta_1=2 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}, \beta_2=-1 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}^2, \beta_3=4 \mathrm{pm} / \mathrm{s}\right)$, where t is time, n and p are constants. At $t=1 \mathrm{~s},\left|\overrightarrow{V_A}\right|=\left|\vec{V}_B\right|$ and velocities $\vec{V}_A$ and $\vec{V}_B$ of the particles are orthogonal to each other. At $t=1 \mathrm{~s}$, the magnitude of angular momentum of particle (A) with respect to the position of particle (B) is $\sqrt{\mathrm{L}} \mathrm{kgm}^2 \mathrm{~s}^{-1}$. The value of L is _______ .
question:
The position vectors of two 1 kg particles, (A) and (B), are given by
$\overrightarrow{\mathrm{r}}_{\mathrm{A}}=\left(\alpha_1 \mathrm{t}^2 \hat{i}+\alpha_2 \mathrm{t} \hat{j}+\alpha_3 \mathrm{t} \hat{k}\right) \mathrm{m}$ and $\overrightarrow{\mathrm{r}}_{\mathrm{B}}=\left(\beta_1 \mathrm{t} \hat{i}+\beta_2 \mathrm{t}^2 \hat{j}+\beta_3 \mathrm{t} \hat{k}\right) \mathrm{m}$, respectively; $\left(\alpha_1=1 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}^2, \alpha_2=3 \mathrm{n} \mathrm{m} / \mathrm{s}, \alpha_3=2 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}, \beta_1=2 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}, \beta_2=-1 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}^2, \beta_3=4 \mathrm{pm} / \mathrm{s}\right)$, where t is time, n and p are constants. At $t=1 \mathrm{~s},\left|\overrightarrow{V_A}\right|=\left|\vec{V}_B\right|$ and velocities $\vec{V}_A$ and $\vec{V}_B$ of the particles are orthogonal to each other. At $t=1 \mathrm{~s}$, the magnitude of angular momentum of particle (A) with respect to the position of particle (B) is $\sqrt{\mathrm{L}} \mathrm{kgm}^2 \mathrm{~s}^{-1}$. The value of L is _______ .
solution:
At $t=1$
$r_{A B}=-1 \hat{i}+(3 n+1) \hat{j}+(2-4 p) \hat{k}$

At $t=1$
$\begin{aligned}
& v_A=2 \hat{i}+3 n \hat{j}+2 \hat{k} \\
& v_B=2 \hat{i}-2 \hat{j}+4 p \hat{k} \\
& \vec{v}_A-\vec{v}_B=0, \quad 4-6 n+8 p=0
\end{aligned}$
$\begin{gathered}\left|v_A\right|=\left|v_B\right| \quad(3 n)^2+4=4+16 p^2 \\ 3 n=-4 p \\ 4+16 p=0 \\ p=-\frac{1}{4}, n=\frac{1}{3} \\ r_{A B}=-\hat{i}+2 \hat{j}+3 \hat{k} \\ v_A=2 \hat{i}+\hat{j}+2 \hat{k} \\ \therefore L=m\left|\vec{r}_{A B} \times \vec{v}_A\right|=90\end{gathered}$

mg-T=ma

RT=Iα

If <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">M</mi></math> is the mass of the rod then its moment of inertia about an axis passing through <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi></math> and perpendicular to the rod :
question: The linear mass density of a thin rod <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>AB</mi></math> of length <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">L</mi></math> varies from <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi></math> to <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">B</mi></math> as <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">λ</mi><mfenced><mi mathvariant="normal">x</mi></mfenced><mo>=</mo><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">λ</mi><mn>0</mn></msub><mfenced><mrow><mn>1</mn><mo>+</mo><mfrac><mi mathvariant="normal">x</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">L</mi></mfrac></mrow></mfenced></math>, where <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">x</mi></math> is the distance from <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi></math>. If <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">M</mi></math> is the mass of the rod then its moment of inertia about an axis passing through <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi></math> and perpendicular to the rod :
solution:
dm=λ.dv

dI=dm.x2

=λ01+xL.x2dx

I=λ0∫01x2+x3Ldx

=λ0x33+x44L0L

=λ0L33+L34

⇒ I=718mL2

subject: Physics
q: Moment of inertia of a rod of mass ' M ' and length 'L' about an axis passing through its center and normal to its length is ' $\alpha$ '. Now the rod is cut into two equal parts and these parts are joined symmetrically to form a cross shape. Moment of inertia of cross about an axis passing through its center and normal to plane containing cross is :
question: Moment of inertia of a rod of mass ' M ' and length 'L' about an axis passing through its center and normal to its length is ' $\alpha$ '. Now the rod is cut into two equal parts and these parts are joined symmetrically to form a cross shape. Moment of inertia of cross about an axis passing through its center and normal to plane containing cross is :
solution:
$\alpha=\frac{\mathrm{M} \ell^2}{12}$
$\begin{aligned} & \alpha^{\prime}=2\left[\frac{\frac{\mathrm{M}}{2}\left(\frac{\ell}{2}\right)^2}{12}\right] \\ & \alpha^{\prime}=\frac{\mathrm{M} \ell^2}{48}=\frac{\alpha}{4}\end{aligned}$
Correct option is (2)

subject: Physics
q: A circular hole of diameter $\mathrm{R}$ is cut from a disc of mass $M$ and radius $R$; the circumference of the cut passes through the centre of the disc. The moment of inertia of the remaining portion of the disc about an axis perpendicular to the disc and passing through its centre is
question: A circular hole of diameter $\mathrm{R}$ is cut from a disc of mass $M$ and radius $R$; the circumference of the cut passes through the centre of the disc. The moment of inertia of the remaining portion of the disc about an axis perpendicular to the disc and passing through its centre is
solution:
M.I. of complete disc about its centre $O$. $$ I_{\mathrm{Total}}=\frac{1}{2} M R^2 $$
Mass of circular hole (removed) $$ =\frac{M}{4} \quad\left(\text { As } M=\pi R^2 t \therefore M \propto R^2\right) $$ M.I. of removed hole about its own axis $$ =\frac{1}{2}\left(\frac{M}{4}\right)\left(\frac{R}{2}\right)^2=\frac{1}{32} M R^2 $$ M.I. of removed hole about $O^{\prime}$ $$ \begin{aligned} I_{\text {removed hole }} & =I_{\mathrm{cm}}+m x^2 \\ & =\frac{M R^2}{32}+\frac{M}{4}\left(\frac{R}{2}\right)^2 \\ & =\frac{M R^2}{32}+\frac{M R^2}{16}=\frac{3 M R^2}{32} \end{aligned} $$ M.I. of complete disc can also be written as $I_{\text {Total }}=I_{\text {removed hole }}+I_{\text {remaining disc }}$ $I_{\text {Total }}=\frac{3 M R^2}{32}+I_{\text {remaining disc }}$ From eq. (i) and (ii), $$ \begin{aligned} & \frac{1}{2} M R^2=\frac{3 M R^2}{32}+I_{\text {remaining disc }} \\ & \Rightarrow I_{\text {remaining disc }} \\ & \quad=\frac{M R^2}{2}-\frac{3 M R^2}{32}=\left(\frac{13}{32}\right) M R^2 \end{aligned} $$

subject: Physics
q: Two discs of moment of inertia <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>I</mi><mn>1</mn></msub><mo>=</mo><mn>4</mn><mo> </mo><mi>kg</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mn>2</mn></msup></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>I</mi><mn>2</mn></msub><mo>=</mo><mn>2</mn><mo> </mo><mi>kg</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mn>2</mn></msup></math> about their central axes & normal to their planes, rotating with angular speeds <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>10</mn><mo> </mo><mi>rad</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><mo>&</mo><mo> </mo><mn>4</mn><mo> </mo><mi>rad</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> respectively are brought into contact face to face with their axe of rotation coincident. The loss in kinetic energy of the system in the process is _________<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">J</mi></math>.
question: Two discs of moment of inertia <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>I</mi><mn>1</mn></msub><mo>=</mo><mn>4</mn><mo> </mo><mi>kg</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mn>2</mn></msup></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>I</mi><mn>2</mn></msub><mo>=</mo><mn>2</mn><mo> </mo><mi>kg</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mn>2</mn></msup></math> about their central axes & normal to their planes, rotating with angular speeds <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>10</mn><mo> </mo><mi>rad</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><mo>&</mo><mo> </mo><mn>4</mn><mo> </mo><mi>rad</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> respectively are brought into contact face to face with their axe of rotation coincident. The loss in kinetic energy of the system in the process is _________<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">J</mi></math>.
solution:
Applying conservation of angular momentum, we get

I1ω1+I2ω2=I1+I2ω0⇒4×10+2×4=4+2ω0⇒ω0=8 rad s-1

Now, initial rotational kinetic energy is E1=12I1ω12+12I2ω22=216 J

and final rotational kinetic energy is

E2=12I1+I2ω02=192 J

Required, energy loss ΔE=E1-E2=24 J

subject: Physics
q: Two identical spheres each of mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mo> </mo><mi>kg</mi></math> and radius <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>50</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math> are fixed at the ends of a light rod so that the separation between the centers is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>150</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math>. Then, moment of inertia of the system about an axis perpendicular to the rod and passing through its middle point is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mi>x</mi><mn>20</mn></mfrac><mo> </mo><mi>kg</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mn>2</mn></msup></math>, where the value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math> is
question: Two identical spheres each of mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mo> </mo><mi>kg</mi></math> and radius <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>50</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math> are fixed at the ends of a light rod so that the separation between the centers is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>150</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math>. Then, moment of inertia of the system about an axis perpendicular to the rod and passing through its middle point is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mi>x</mi><mn>20</mn></mfrac><mo> </mo><mi>kg</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mn>2</mn></msup></math>, where the value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math> is
solution:
Using parallel axis theorem, we can write total moment of inertia as, I=25mR2+md2×2

⇒I=225×2×122+2×342=5320 kg m2

Therefore, x=53.
